title: A Dream of Fair Women
content:
I read, before my eyelids dropt their shade,
ï¿½The Legend of Good Women,ï¿½ long ago
Sung by the morning star of song, who made
His music heard below;

Dan Chaucer, the first warbler, whose sweet breath
Preluded those melodious bursts that fill
The spacious times of great Elizabeth
With sounds that echo still.

And, for a while, the knowledge of his art
Held me above the subject, as strong gales
Hold swollen clouds from raining, thoï¿½ my heart,
Brimful of those wild tales,

Charged both mine eyes with tears. In every land
I saw, wherever light illumineth,
Beauty and anguish walking hand in hand
The downward slope to death.

Those far-renowned brides of ancient song
Peopled the hollow dark, like burning stars,
And I heard sounds of insult, shame, and wrong,
And trumpets blown for wars;

And clattering flints batterï¿½d with clanging hoofs;
And I saw crowds in columnï¿½d sanctuaries;
And forms that passï¿½d at windows and on roofs
Of marble palaces;

Corpses across the threshold; heroes tall
Dislodging pinnacle and parapet
Upon the tortoise creeping to the wall;
Lances in ambush set;

And high shrine-doors burst throï¿½ with heated blasts
That run before the fluttering tongues of fire;
White surf wind-scatterï¿½d over sails and masts,
And ever climbing higher;

Squadrons and squares of men in brazen plates,
Scaffolds, still sheets of water, divers woes,
Ranges of glimmering vaults with iron grates,
And hushï¿½d seraglios.

So shape chased shape as swift as, when to land
Bluster the winds and tides the self-same way,
Crisp foam-flakes scud along the level sand,
Torn from the fringe of spray.

I started once, or seemï¿½d to start in pain,
Resolved on noble things, and strove to speak,
As when a great thought strikes along the brain,
And flushes all the cheek.

And once my arm was lifted to hew down
A cavalier from off his saddle-bow,
That bore a lady from a leaguerï¿½d town;
And then, I know not how,

All those sharp fancies, by down-lapsing thought
Streamï¿½d onward, lost their edges, and did creep
Rollï¿½d on each other, rounded, smoothï¿½d, and brought
Into the gulfs of sleep.

At last methought that I had wanderï¿½d far
In an old wood: fresh-washï¿½d in coolest dew
The maiden splendours of the morning star
Shook in the stedfast blue.

Enormous elm-tree-boles did stoop and lean
Upon the dusky brushwood underneath
Their broad curved branches, fledged with clearest green,
New from its silken sheath.

The dim red morn had died, her journey done,
And with dead lips smiled at the twilight plain,
Half-fallï¿½n across the threshold of the sun,
Never to rise again.

There was no motion in the dumb dead air,
Not any song of bird or sound of rill;
Gross darkness of the inner sepulchre
Is not so deadly still

As that wide forest. Growths of jasmine turnï¿½d
Their humid arms festooning tree to tree,
And at the root throï¿½ lush green grasses burnï¿½d
The red anemone.

I knew the flowers, I knew the leaves, I knew
The tearful glimmer of the languid dawn
On those long, rank, dark wood-walks drenchï¿½d in dew,
Leading from lawn to lawn.

The smell of violets, hidden in the green,
Pourï¿½d back into my empty soul and frame
The times when I remember to have been
Joyful and free from blame.

And from within me a clear under-tone
Thrillï¿½d throï¿½ mine ears in that unblissful clime,
ï¿½Pass freely throï¿½: the wood is all thine own,
Until the end of time.ï¿½

At length I saw a lady within call,
Stiller than chisellï¿½d marble, standing there;
A daughter of the gods, divinely tall,
And most divinely fair.

Her loveliness with shame and with surprise
Froze my swift speech: she turning on my face
The star-like sorrows of immortal eyes,
Spoke slowly in her place.

ï¿½I had great beauty: ask thou not my name:
No one can be more wise than destiny.
Many drew swords and died. Whereï¿½er I came
I brought calamity.ï¿½

ï¿½No marvel, sovereign lady: in fair field
Myself for such a face had boldly died,ï¿½
I answerï¿½d free; and turning I appealï¿½d
To one that stood beside.

But she, with sick and scornful looks averse,
To her full height her stately stature draws;
ï¿½My youth,ï¿½ she said, ï¿½was blasted with a curse:
This woman was the cause.

ï¿½I was cut off from hope in that sad place,
Which men callï¿½d Aulis in those iron years:
My father held his hand upon his face;
I, blinded with my tears,

ï¿½Still strove to speak: my voice was thick with sighs
As in a dream. Dimly I could descry
The stern black-bearded kings with wolfish eyes,
Waiting to see me die.

ï¿½The high masts flickerï¿½d as they lay afloat;
The crowds, the temples, waverï¿½d, and the shore;
The bright death quiverï¿½d at the victimï¿½s throat;
Touchï¿½d; and I knew no more.ï¿½

Whereto the other with a downward brow:
ï¿½I would the white cold heavy-plunging foam,
Whirlï¿½d by the wind, had rollï¿½d me deep below,
Then when I left my home.ï¿½

Her slow full words sank throï¿½ the silence drear,
As thunder-drops fall on a sleeping sea:
Sudden I heard a voice that cried, ï¿½Come here,
That I may look on thee.ï¿½

I turning saw, throned on a flowery rise,
One sitting on a crimson scarf unrollï¿½d;
A queen, with swarthy cheeks and bold black eyes,
Brow-bound with burning gold.

She, flashing forth a haughty smile, began:
ï¿½I governï¿½d men by change, and so I swayï¿½d
All moods. ï¿½Tis long since I have seen a man.
Once, like the moon, I made

ï¿½The ever-shifting currents of the blood
According to my humour ebb and flow.
I have no men to govern in this wood:
That makes my only woe.

ï¿½Nayï¿½yet it chafes me that I could not bend
One will; nor tame and tutor with mine eye
That dull cold-blooded Cï¿½sar. Prythee, friend,
Where is Mark Antony?

ï¿½The man, my lover, with whom I rode sublime
On Fortuneï¿½s neck: we sat as God by God:
The Nilus would have risen before his time
And flooded at our nod.

ï¿½We drank the Libyan Sun to sleep, and lit
Lamps which out-burnï¿½d Canopus. O my life
In Egypt! O the dalliance and the wit,
The flattery and the strife,

ï¿½And the wild kiss, when fresh from warï¿½s alarms,
My Hercules, my Roman Antony,
My mailed Bacchus leapt into my arms,
Contented there to die!

ï¿½And there he died: and when I heard my name
Sighï¿½d forth with life I would not brook my fear
Of the other: with a worm I balkï¿½d his fame.
What else was left? look here!ï¿½

(With that she tore her robe apart, and half
The polishï¿½d argent of her breast to sight
Laid bare. Thereto she pointed with a laugh,
Showing the aspickï¿½s bite.)

ï¿½I died a Queen. The Roman soldier found
Me lying dead, my crown about my brows,
A name for ever!ï¿½lying robed and crownï¿½d,
Worthy a Roman spouse.ï¿½

Her warbling voice, a lyre of widest range
Struck by all passion, did fall down and glance
From tone to tone, and glided throï¿½ all change
Of liveliest utterance.

When she made pause I knew not for delight;
Because with sudden motion from the ground
She raised her piercing orbs, and fillï¿½d with light
The interval of sound.

Still with their fires Love tipt his keenest darts;
As once they drew into two burning rings
All beams of Love, melting the mighty hearts
Of captains and of kings.

Slowly my sense undazzled. Then I heard
A noise of some one coming throï¿½ the lawn,
And singing clearer than the crested bird
That claps his wings at dawn.

ï¿½The torrent brooks of hallowï¿½d Israel
From craggy hollows pouring, late and soon,
Sound all night long, in falling throï¿½ the dell,
Far-heard beneath the moon.

ï¿½The balmy moon of blessed Israel
Floods all the deep-blue gloom with beams divine:
All night the splinterï¿½d crags that wall the dell
With spires of silver shine.ï¿½

As one that museth where broad sunshine laves
The lawn by some cathedral, throï¿½ the door
Hearing the holy organ rolling waves
Of sound on roof and floor

Within, and anthem sung, is charmï¿½d and tied
To where he stands,ï¿½so stood I, when that flow
Of music left the lips of her that died
To save her fatherï¿½s vow;

The daughter of the warrior Gileadite,
A maiden pure; as when she went along
From Mizpehï¿½s towerï¿½d gate with welcome light,
With timbrel and with song.

My words leapt forth: ï¿½Heaven heads the count of crimes
With that wild oath.ï¿½ She renderï¿½d answer high:
ï¿½Not so, nor once alone; a thousand times
I would be born and die.

ï¿½Single I grew, like some green plant, whose root
Creeps to the garden water-pipes beneath,
Feeding the flower; but ere my flower to fruit
Changed, I was ripe for death.

ï¿½My God, my land, my fatherï¿½these did move
Me from my bliss of life, that Nature gave,
Lowerï¿½d softly with a threefold cord of love
Down to a silent grave.

ï¿½And I went mourning, ï¿½No fair Hebrew boy
Shall smile away my maiden blame among
The Hebrew mothersï¿½ï¿½emptied of all joy,
Leaving the dance and song,

ï¿½Leaving the olive-gardens far below,
Leaving the promise of my bridal bower,
The valleys of grape-loaded vines that glow
Beneath the battled tower.

ï¿½The light white cloud swam over us. Anon
We heard the lion roaring from his den;
We saw the large white stars rise one by one,
Or, from the darkenï¿½d glen,

ï¿½Saw God divide the night with flying flame,
And thunder on the everlasting hills.
I heard Him, for He spake, and grief became
A solemn scorn of ills.

ï¿½When the next moon was rollï¿½d into the sky,
Strength came to me that equallï¿½d my desire.
How beautiful a thing it was to die
For God and for my sire!

ï¿½It comforts me in this one thought to dwell,
That I subdued me to my fatherï¿½s will;
Because the kiss he gave me, ere I fell,
Sweetens the spirit still.

ï¿½Moreover it is written that my race
Hewï¿½d Ammon, hip and thigh, from Aroer
On Arnon unto Minneth.ï¿½ Here her face
Glowï¿½d, as I lookï¿½d at her.

She lockï¿½d her lips: she left me where I stood:
ï¿½Glory to God,ï¿½ she sang, and past afar,
Thridding the sombre boskage of the wood,
Toward the morning-star.

Losing her carol I stood pensively,
As one that from a casement leans his head,
When midnight bells cease ringing suddenly,
And the old year is dead.

ï¿½Alas! alas!ï¿½ a low voice, full of care,
Murmurï¿½d beside me: ï¿½Turn and look on me:
I am that Rosamond, whom men call fair,
If what I was I be.

ï¿½Would I had been some maiden coarse and poor!
O me, that I should ever see the light!
Those dragon eyes of angerï¿½d Eleanor
Do hunt me, day and night.ï¿½

She ceased in tears, fallen from hope and trust:
To whom the Egyptian: ï¿½O, you tamely died!
You should have clung to Fulviaï¿½s waist, and thrust
The dagger throï¿½ her side.ï¿½

With that sharp sound the white dawnï¿½s creeping beams,
Stolï¿½n to my brain, dissolved the mystery
Of folded sleep. The captain of my dreams
Ruled in the eastern sky.

Morn broadenï¿½d on the borders of the dark,
Ere I saw her, who claspï¿½d in her last trance
Her murderï¿½d fatherï¿½s head, or Joan of Arc,
A light of ancient France;

Or her who knew that Love can vanquish Death,
Who kneeling, with one arm about her king,
Drew forth the poison with her balmy breath,
Sweet as new buds in Spring.

No memory labours longer from tbe deep
Gold-mines of thought to lift the hidden ore
That glimpses, moving up, than I from sleep
To gather and tell oï¿½er

Each little sound and sight. With what dull pain
Compassï¿½d, how eagerly I sought to strike
Into that wondrous track of dreams again!
But no two dreams are like.

As when a soul laments, which hath been blest,
Desiring what is mingled with past years,
In yearnings that can never be exprest
By signs or groans or tears;

Because all words, thoï¿½ cullï¿½d with choicest art,
Failing to give the bitter of the sweet,
Wither beneath the palate, and the heart
Faints, faded by its heat.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
